easy.question: What is ‘hate’ under the policy?
easy.answer: Any criminal offence perceived by the victim or any other person to be motivated by hostility or prejudice toward a personal characteristic. :contentReference[oaicite:23]{index=23}

easy.question: Which five centrally monitored strands are listed?
easy.answer: Race/ethnicity, religion/beliefs, sexual orientation, disability, and gender reassignment/transgender identity. (The policy notes other characteristics like age/gender may also be targeted.) :contentReference[oaicite:24]{index=24}

easy.question: Who is covered by the policy?
easy.answer: Customers of all tenures living in homes owned/managed by Midland Heart and, where a customer or associated person perpetrates hate, staff and contractors are also covered. :contentReference[oaicite:25]{index=25}

easy.question: How quickly are hate cases triaged and allocated?
easy.answer: Hate is treated with the highest priority and is triaged by the Customer Hub and allocated for follow-up the next working day. :contentReference[oaicite:26]{index=26}

easy.question: What immediate property-related actions can be taken after a hate incident?
easy.answer: Emergency repairs within 24 hours and removal of offensive graffiti within 1 working day; additional security may be provided in line with risk assessment. :contentReference[oaicite:27]{index=27}

easy.question: Can third parties report incidents on someone’s behalf?
easy.answer: Yes—reports are accepted from third parties (e.g., advice centres, places of worship). Written consent is required to discuss case details with a representative; MPs/Councillors do not require written consent. :contentReference[oaicite:28]{index=28}

easy.question: Through which channels can hate be reported and how is privacy handled on social media?
easy.answer: Any channel—staff, phone, email, social media; social media receives a private reply to protect confidentiality. :contentReference[oaicite:29]{index=29}

easy.question: What is Midland Heart’s role as a Third-Party Reporting Centre?
easy.answer: It can report incidents to Police on behalf of victims with consent (or anonymously for intelligence) and encourages reporting to reveal true scale and support victims. :contentReference[oaicite:30]{index=30}

medium.question: A tenant receives threatening notes with slurs. What early steps and support are required?
medium.answer: Treat as highest priority; triage/allocate next working day; risk-assess; agree a tailored action plan and update method/frequency; provide emergency repairs/security if needed; coordinate with partners. :contentReference[oaicite:31]{index=31}

medium.question: A community group emails details for a victim who fears contacting Midland Heart. How do you proceed?
medium.answer: Log the report; contact the victim via their preferred safe channel; obtain written consent before discussing case details with the group; proceed with risk assessment and tailored support. :contentReference[oaicite:32]{index=32}

medium.question: When is safeguarding triggered in hate cases?
medium.answer: Whenever there are concerns that an adult or child is at risk of harm or abuse; reports are made to Police/Local Authority and multi-agency protocols followed. :contentReference[oaicite:33]{index=33}

medium.question: What tools can be used from early intervention to enforcement?
medium.answer: A full range from advisory/early interventions to tenancy enforcement and legal powers in serious cases, proportionate to harm and in line with the ASB Policy. :contentReference[oaicite:34]{index=34}

medium.question: How is case progress communicated to victims?
medium.answer: A named officer agrees the method and frequency of updates and keeps the victim informed throughout. :contentReference[oaicite:35]{index=35}

medium.question: When can a hate case be closed?
medium.answer: When risks are sufficiently mitigated, issues are resolved, no further action is possible without the victim’s help, another agency is leading, or there have been no further reports—closure is discussed with the victim. :contentReference[oaicite:36]{index=36}

medium.question: How is accessibility ensured for reporting and engagement?
medium.answer: Reasonable adjustments are offered (no fixed list), agreed case-by-case—e.g., interpretation, tailored communication—so people can report and engage. :contentReference[oaicite:37]{index=37}

medium.question: What external review option exists if a victim is unhappy with the wider handling of ASB/hate?
medium.answer: They (or any member of the public) can request an ASB Case Review via the local partnership; Midland Heart will cooperate and consider recommendations. :contentReference[oaicite:38]{index=38}

hard.question: A tenant experiences escalating hate graffiti, online abuse, and threats in the stairwell. Design a response (risk, property measures, evidence, partnership, and comms).
hard.answer: Treat as highest priority; risk-assess and agree a plan with the victim; remove offensive graffiti within 1 working day and complete emergency repairs/security; gather evidence (statements, images, CCTV where applicable); work with Police/partners using information-sharing protocols; agree the update method/frequency and support needs; consider tenancy enforcement for perpetrators and multi-agency panels where appropriate. :contentReference[oaicite:39]{index=39}

hard.question: A hate incident is reported by a third party who refuses to share the victim’s details. What can be done?
hard.answer: Record the incident and, if consent can’t be obtained, share anonymised intelligence with Police as a Third-Party Reporting Centre; encourage the third party to support the victim to contact us; where there’s safeguarding risk, escalate appropriately. :contentReference[oaicite:40]{index=40}

hard.question: The alleged perpetrator is not a Midland Heart customer. What action is possible?
hard.answer: Work with relevant agencies (Police/LA/others) to determine appropriate action and who should lead; continue supporting the victim and use tenancy-related powers where applicable. :contentReference[oaicite:41]{index=41}

hard.question: How does the policy balance enforcement with support for perpetrators when appropriate?
hard.answer: It uses proportionate enforcement in serious cases but can also provide support/referrals for perpetrators if needs are identified and services exist—aiming for sustainable outcomes. :contentReference[oaicite:42]{index=42}

hard.question: What data-sharing and consent rules apply when an advocacy organisation requests case details?
hard.answer: Written consent from the tenant is required to share personal information with representatives; MPs/Councillors don’t need written consent; otherwise, share under legal/safeguarding gateways only. :contentReference[oaicite:43]{index=43}

hard.question: Outline a learning and prevention loop from hate cases.
hard.answer: Review cases and use tenant insights to understand prevention; partner with statutory agencies on strategic responses (including homelessness impacts) and run campaigns to encourage reporting. :contentReference[oaicite:44]{index=44}
